title: Usage of attributes clocker and clock_enable
body:
I am really struggling with the clocker and clock_enable attributes.

Let's start with clock_enable: Reading V3Order all this seems to achieve is ignore a the dependency in combinational logic on the annotated signal. Under what circumstance is this useful (and does not kill you by ignoring the dependency)? I cannot think of a use case.

As of clocker: This seems to do two things:
1. Make some dependencies [as defined by whatever `OrderClkMarkVisitor` propagates this attribute to, which is by no means comprehensive] feeding this variable through combinational logic be a hard edge, instead of the a cutable one. The point of this is that we don't have a change detect on the generated clock, which seems fair, but Verilator could compute this itself without any annotation.
2. Similar to clock_enable, ignore all *other* variable dependencies in combinational logic driving the clocker. I.e.: as fi all other signals feeding the clocker had clock_enable.

For the "let's not have the generated clock circular" part (which seems like just a performance thing?), shouldn't we just change all edges that feed a sensitivity list (i.e.: clocks) to non-cutable in the V3Order dependency graph. If we end up with a hard cycle, throw up hands, or warn and automatically pick an edge to cut in some intelligent way?

As of the ignored dependencies, when are these useful? They seem always unsafe.
